title: Go Big Or Go Home

jokes.text: Giddyup! Giddyup! / I am the pants queen!
jokes.punchline: Leslie shouting random enthusiastic phrases like 'pants queen'
jokes.explanation: Perfect character introduction - Leslie's manic enthusiasm condensed into absurd declarations. Economy is excellent.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: Pants queen!

jokes.text: Cut it out, Tom. / It never gets old.
jokes.setup: Leslie asks Tom to stop something
jokes.punchline: Tom's deadpan response that he enjoys annoying her
jokes.explanation: Simple workplace dynamic joke, efficiently delivered but not particularly memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Tom, you have to stop doing that.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: But Leslie, I enjoy annoying you. It's like my favorite hobby. Well, that and online shopping for things I don't need.

jokes.text: Tommy Timberlake
jokes.punchline: He calls himself 'Tommy Timberlake' while singing
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tom character moment - his delusions of grandeur in two words. Cheap celebrity reference but fits character.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I'm Tommy Timberlake!

jokes.text: My mom is Puerto Rican. That's why I'm so lively and colorful.
jokes.punchline: Deadpan delivery of explanation for personality that's opposite of her actual demeanor
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character introduction - April's deadpan sarcasm about being 'lively and colorful' when she's anything but. Perfect irony.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I have the same dead, expressionless stare and general apathy that I've had since childhood. I'm not sure why. I think it started when I was born and the doctor slapped me, and I thought, 'okay, whatever.'

jokes.text: Nailed it.
jokes.punchline: Andy proudly declaring 'Nailed it' about something mundane
jokes.explanation: Classic Andy - overconfident about simple tasks. Great economy, perfect character beat.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Nailed it.

jokes.text: Scientists believe that the first human being who will live 150 years has already been born. I believe I am that human being.
jokes.setup: Chris delivering exposition about government shutdown
jokes.punchline: His narcissistic belief he'll live 150 years
jokes.explanation: Perfect Chris introduction - his extreme optimism and self-regard delivered with complete sincerity.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: I've been training my body like a machine. I do 200 sit-ups every morning, I only eat foods that are genetically similar to foods that humans ate 10,000 years ago, and I've eliminated all processed sugars and refined carbohydrates from my diet. So when this shutdown is over, I'll still be here. In fact, I'll probably live to be 150 years old.

jokes.text: Bully.
jokes.setup: Leslie enthusiastically announces they're back to work
jokes.punchline: Ron's understated, old-fashioned response
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron - archaic vocabulary delivered with stoic understatement. Single word does all the work.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: We're back to work!

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Dear God, no.

jokes.text: The bankrupt government of Pawnee has been shut down all summer, so it's been three months of no work, no meetings, no memos, no late nights, nothing. I wouldn't wish it on my worst enemy.
jokes.setup: Leslie lists all the work activities that have been suspended
jokes.punchline: She considers this paradise to be torture
jokes.explanation: Perfect Leslie - what others would see as vacation is hell to her. Great character-driven irony.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: All work activities have been suspended: no meetings, no emails, no reports, no committees.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: This is paradise.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: This is torture.

jokes.text: I have goose bumps. Feel.
jokes.setup: Leslie expressing excitement about returning to work
jokes.punchline: Physical comedy of her forcing someone to feel her goose bumps
jokes.explanation: Leslie's physical enthusiasm and boundary-crossing behavior. Simple but effective character moment.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Feel my goose bumps! Feel them!

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Leslie, what—

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: No, just feel them. This is how excited I am to be back at work.

jokes.text: And that's why they call me Prince Charming. Because I always find the glass slipper for my Cinderella. / These are way too tight. / Well, the real Cinderella didn't have hippo feet.
jokes.setup: Tom's romantic metaphor about finding the perfect fit
jokes.punchline: Reality check that the shoes don't fit, followed by insult about 'hippo feet'
jokes.explanation: Good escalation from romantic metaphor to harsh reality. Tom's delusions punctured effectively.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Love is like finding the perfect pair of shoes. You search and search, and when you finally find them, they fit just right.

dialogue.speaker: Unknown
dialogue.line: That's actually really sweet, Tom.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, I tried those shoes on you and they don't fit. You got hippo feet.

jokes.text: Jeremy! Suck it!
jokes.punchline: Random aggressive shout at unknown Jeremy
jokes.explanation: Tom's random aggression fits character but structure is weak. Just weird for weird's sake.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Hey Leslie.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: HEY JEREMY, YOU SUCK!

jokes.text: By the way, I've been giving away free sports bras to the girls at Hot Dog on a Stick. Look, we can dance all day, but it's time to step up. Are you buying 4,000 rubber nipples from me or not?
jokes.punchline: Escalates to increasingly absurd business proposition about rubber nipples
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tom - random business schemes with sexual undertones. Quotable absurdity.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: You know what? Sports bras are genius. We should make a sports bra for men.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Actually, no. We should make sports bras for dogs. Think about it—dogs running around, their nipples bouncing everywhere.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: But here's where it gets good. We make the bras out of rubber. Rubber nipples. For when the dogs want to feel... protected.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: We call it 'Pup Armor.' I'm telling you, this is the next big thing. We'll be millionaires.

jokes.text: Jerry. / Oh, my gosh. Look who's here. Leslie. / The nightmare's over, Jerry. We're going back to work. / You're not going to need this anymore. (SPLASH)
jokes.setup: Leslie greeting Jerry and saying the nightmare is over
jokes.punchline: Physical comedy of Leslie throwing something into water
jokes.explanation: Leslie's dramatic gesture fits character. Physical comedy moment but not especially memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: The nightmare is over!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What happened?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I threw it in the water.

jokes.text: I called shotgun. Everybody heard me.
jokes.punchline: Appeal to witnesses about calling shotgun
jokes.explanation: Generic situational humor. Familiar premise without fresh angle.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I called shotgun.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: That was like three hours ago.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Shotgun doesn't expire. It's like a gift card to the front seat.

jokes.text: Well, I am usually not one for speeches. So, goodbye.
jokes.setup: Ron beginning what sounds like a speech
jokes.punchline: Immediately ending it with 'goodbye'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron moment - subverts speech expectations with extreme brevity. Excellent structure and character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I have something to say.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Goodbye.

jokes.text: Make the world's biggest pizza. / No, make this town fun for the people who live here. / Fine, but after that, the pizza is our top priority. / No, it's not. / We're getting pizza?
jokes.setup: Leslie trying to give motivational speech
jokes.punchline: Andy's complete focus on pizza derailing the moment
jokes.explanation: Good escalation with Andy's food obsession derailing Leslie's serious moment. Character-driven humor.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: You know, I really believe in each and every one of you. Together, we can accomplish anything we set our minds to.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: That's great, Leslie. Hey, did anyone else hear that? I think someone just ordered pizza in the break room.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Andy, I'm trying to inspire the team here.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I know, I know, but is it pepperoni? Because if it's pepperoni, I need to be there right now.

jokes.text: April, hey, it's me, Andy Dwyer. This is like the 200th message I've left you, without a response. So, if you're trying to tell me something, I do not know what it is because you won't call me back.
jokes.punchline: His complete obliviousness to obvious rejection
jokes.explanation: Good cringe humor - Andy's romantic obliviousness creates sympathetic discomfort.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Hey April, it's Andy. I was just thinking about you and I wanted to call and see what you're up to. Maybe we could hang out sometime? Like, I don't know, get some food or something? Anyway, just let me know. I'm pretty free most nights. Okay, bye!

jokes.text: So, that just means that we're in maintenance mode. / Yeah! Jerry!
jokes.punchline: Andy enthusiastically cheering for Jerry for no reason
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection - Andy's random enthusiasm punctures serious moment. Efficient delivery.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: I'm going to be very clear about what you can and cannot do in this position.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Oh my god, yes! Go Jerry!

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: What? I haven't even started yet.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I know, I'm just getting ahead of it. That's my guy right there!

jokes.text: And quite frankly, throw things at you and call you names. Like Turd-boy. Whatever.
jokes.setup: Ben explaining why basketball league is necessary
jokes.punchline: His deadpan delivery of being called 'Turd-boy'
jokes.explanation: Ben's deadpan delivery of juvenile insult he endured. Good contrast between professional demeanor and stupid nickname.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: I was called 'Turd-boy' in middle school. For three years. Do you know what that does to a person? That's why we need this basketball league.

jokes.text: Under my tutelage, you will grow from boys into men, from men into gladiators, and from gladiators into Swansons.
jokes.punchline: Escalation ending with ultimate goal of becoming 'Swansons'
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation with perfect Ron narcissism. 'Swansons' as ultimate achievement is brilliant character moment.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I have nothing but respect for basketball players. You're strong, you're disciplined, and you understand the importance of hard work. That's why I'm here today to propose something radical: we change your team name from the Sweetbriar Slugs to something with real power and dignity. The Swansons.

jokes.text: Behold! The Swanson Pyramid of Greatness.
jokes.punchline: The absurdity of a personal pyramid of greatness named after himself
jokes.explanation: Iconic Ron moment - visual gag combining self-importance with pseudo-educational presentation. Became cultural touchstone.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I present to you the Ron Swanson Pyramid of Greatness. Each level represents a different aspect of greatness.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: At the base, we have capitalism. The engine of achievement.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Next, we have honor, integrity, and good scotch.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: At the top, we have me.

jokes.text: Capitalism. God's way of determining who is smart, and who is poor.
jokes.punchline: His brutal oversimplification of capitalism
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron - libertarian philosophy delivered with religious certainty. Dark humor in the simplistic worldview.

dialogue.speaker: Ron Swanson
dialogue.line: There are three ways to accomplish something: the right way, the wrong way, and the way that I do it.

dialogue.speaker: Ron Swanson
dialogue.line: Capitalism: God's way of determining who is smart and who is poor.

jokes.text: Crying. Acceptable at funerals and the Grand Canyon.
jokes.punchline: Oddly specific acceptable crying scenarios
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Ron moment - hyperspecific rules for emotion. Grand Canyon inclusion is perfectly absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: There are only three acceptable times for a man to cry: when he's chpping onions, when he's watching the great films, and when a trout bites his hand off.

jokes.text: Fish, for sport only, not for meat. Fish meat is practically a vegetable.
jokes.punchline: Contradictory logic about fish being vegetables
jokes.explanation: Good Ron logic - carnivore who dismisses fish as 'practically a vegetable.' Absurd but character-consistent.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Fish are vegetables.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: They don't have legs, so they can't run away. That means they're vegetables.

jokes.text: Haircuts. There are three acceptable haircuts. High and tight. Crew cut. Buzz cut. Are the scissors broken in your house, son?
jokes.punchline: Insult to kid about his hair
jokes.explanation: Good Ron moment - rigid rules followed by personal insult to child. Character-appropriate rudeness.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Your hair looks like a bird's nest made of disappointment and broken dreams.

jokes.text: We literally can't buy... (BLEEP)
jokes.punchline: Her frustration causing her to swear, then apologize
jokes.explanation: Leslie's rare loss of composure followed by immediate politeness. Character-appropriate but predictable.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I can't believe we have to cut the budget again. This is absolutely ridiculous!

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Son of a b—

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, I apologize. That was inappropriate.

jokes.text: I'm not a paper pusher, Ann. I need to be out in the streets, planting trees and cracking skulls.
jokes.punchline: Unexpected escalation to 'cracking skulls'
jokes.explanation: Good escalation - Leslie's earnestness about public service turns violent. Unexpected for her character.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: I work hard. I play hard. I give everything 110%. And when someone gets in my way, I crack skulls.

jokes.text: No. Damn! Sorry, Leslie.
jokes.setup: Chris asked if anything can be done about budget
jokes.punchline: His immediate enthusiastic disappointment followed by apology
jokes.explanation: Chris's extreme enthusiasm even for bad news. Brief but character-consistent.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: Well, we could always try to get a grant, or maybe reallocate some funds from another department. There are actually several options we could explore that might—

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, I got excited about budget solutions for a second there. That was inappropriate.

jokes.text: Save it. Okay? I know you don't have faith in me and my department, and that's fine. But don't expect me to sit here and chitchat with you. / All right. Fair enough. Go the other way. / But I'm just going to that... / All right, I'II...
jokes.setup: Leslie confronting Chris about lack of support
jokes.punchline: Physical comedy of them both trying to walk same direction
jokes.explanation: Basic physical comedy of directional confusion. Functional but not memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Chris, I can't believe you're not supporting me on this initiative!

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: Leslie, I am supporting you, I'm just trying to be fiscally responsible.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Well, maybe if you moved out of my way, I could actually get somewhere!

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: I'm trying to move! You keep going the same direction I'm going!

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Because you keep blocking me!

jokes.text: Because I thought we had a great time the night you got drunk and kissed me. And you did use your tongue.
jokes.setup: Chris asking why Ann won't date him
jokes.punchline: Ann's uncomfortable oversharing about their kiss
jokes.explanation: Cringe humor from Ann's TMI about kissing. Creates awkwardness but not particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: Why won't you go out with me?

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Chris, that kiss was like kissing a tall, blond, athletic who's-who of disappointment. Your mouth tasted like you'd been eating a tire fire. And when you tried to grab my butt, you missed and got my hip. I've never felt less attractive in my entire life.

jokes.text: Great. Let me ask you one question. Would you be cool doing things that a prostitute does? Minus the money? / Definitely yes, then.
jokes.setup: Leslie asking if Ann would do prostitute things minus money
jokes.punchline: Ann's enthusiastic agreement when money is removed
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection - Ann agrees to prostitution when payment is removed. Surprising response.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: So you're saying you would do all those things... just not for money?

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Yes! Absolutely!

jokes.text: I'd say my coaching style is centered around fundamentals, with an emphasis on fun. Hey, watch this. Go get it. Yeah! And a second emphasis on mental.
jokes.punchline: Demonstration reveals he's just throwing ball away for kids to chase
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection - Andy's serious coaching talk undermined by treating kids like dogs fetching.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: You know, my coaching philosophy is all about building character and teaching kids the fundamentals of the game.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: It's not about winning, it's about the journey.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Watch this.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Hey kids, go get it!

jokes.text: But every time I look one of these kids in the eyes and he calls me Coach... That's how I know I agreed to be a coach.
jokes.setup: Andy building to inspirational moment about coaching
jokes.punchline: Anticlimax - he only knows he's a coach because kids call him coach
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Andy moment - sets up inspirational speech but delivers circular logic. Perfect character obliviousness.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: You know, being a coach has really changed my life. I wake up every morning and I think about all these kids looking up to me, depending on me to guide them, to shape their futures. And I realize that I have this incredible responsibility to be someone they can believe in, someone they can trust, someone who—

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Wait, how do I know I'm a coach?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: The kids call me Coach.

jokes.text: There's no coin toss in basketball. / Are those women's sneakers? / Yes, they are, Ron. You know what? They fit better, I got an employee discount, and the best part is no one can tell.
jokes.setup: Ron pointing out Tom's women's sneakers
jokes.punchline: Tom's elaborate justification ending with 'no one can tell'
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Tom's vanity and elaborate justifications. Irony in 'no one can tell' when Ron immediately noticed.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Are those women's sneakers?

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: They are, but hear me out. Women's sneakers are designed with a narrower heel and a more tapered toe box. Plus, they come in way better colors and patterns. And they're often cheaper. So I get superior comfort and style at a lower price point. It's just smart shopping. And honestly, no one can tell.

jokes.text: Match point. Touchdown. Et cetera.
jokes.setup: Tom trying to start basketball game
jokes.punchline: Using wrong sports terminology and giving up with 'et cetera'
jokes.explanation: Tom's sports ignorance and lazy 'et cetera' finish. Efficient character moment.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Alright everyone, let's get this basketball game started. First we need to do the coin toss, or whatever—the thing where you throw the ball up and people jump for it. Then we run back and forth, and try to put the ball in the hoop. There's a guy with a whistle. And at the end, whoever has the most points wins, obviously. And then there's... timeouts, and fouls, and traveling, and the three-point line, and the slam dunk contest, and et cetera, et cetera.

jokes.text: Okay. I like what you're wearing, but I need it to be 300% sexier. Do you have any of those shirts that look wet all the time? Or, like, a metal bikini?
jokes.punchline: Escalating to completely impractical 'metal bikini'
jokes.explanation: Good escalation - Leslie's complete misunderstanding of seduction leads to metal bikini suggestion.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Okay Ann, here's what you do. First, wear something that shows you're interested in him. Maybe a nice dress.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Then escalate to something a little more daring. A short skirt, maybe some heels.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Then a leather jacket. Then a metal bikini. Then a full metal bikini with spikes.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: By that point he'll either be completely into you or calling animal control.

jokes.text: For dinner? / Well, what's sexy food? Asparagus? / No, you know what's sexy? Turkey chili.
jokes.punchline: Leslie's declaration that turkey chili is sexy
jokes.explanation: Perfect Leslie - earnest belief that turkey chili is seductive. Absurd but character-driven.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Turkey chili is sexy.

jokes.text: Ann Perkins. You are wonderful and amazing and I'm happy to be here with you. / Thank you, Chris. I'm wearing a tuxedo vest with no shirt on underneath.
jokes.punchline: Ann's awkward response about tuxedo vest
jokes.explanation: Role-play comedy with Ann's awkward seduction attempt. Moderately funny but predictable.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ann, I'm Chris now. What do you think of my tuxedo vest?

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: It's very... efficient.

jokes.text: And... Oops, my vest popped open. Just like the budget needs to pop open and you need to pour it into my Parks Department.
jokes.punchline: Terrible transition from vest to budget metaphor
jokes.explanation: Perfect Leslie - terrible at seduction, immediately pivots to work. Awful transition is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I'm like a vest. I go over everything.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: A vest. It's versatile. You can wear it in any season. And it complements whatever's underneath.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Leslie, what are you doing?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I'm seducing you with my vest knowledge. Like the Parks Department, I bring added value to your life. I'm essential but also optional, like a vest.

jokes.text: I love dates. I love connecting with someone. I love engaging them. I love being surprised by them. I have never had a bad date. They've all been either great or phenomenally great.
jokes.punchline: His extreme optimism about all dates being great
jokes.explanation: Good Chris moment - his relentless positivity about everything including dating. Character-consistent.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: Every date is a good date. Even the bad dates are good dates because you learn something about yourself.

jokes.text: Nurses are the most undervalued members of our society by far. I think all of you should make as much money as the CEO of Google.
jokes.punchline: Extreme escalation to Google CEO salary
jokes.explanation: Chris's extreme complimenting escalated to absurd salary comparison. Character-appropriate but predictable.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: Nurses are incredible. They work so hard, they care so much. I think they should be paid like CEOs.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: Actually, they should be paid more than CEOs. They should be paid like the CEO of Google.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: No wait, they should be paid like the CEO of Google, plus stock options, plus a private jet, plus naming rights to a country.

jokes.text: I was born with a blood disorder. And my parents were told that I had three weeks to live. And here I still am, some 2,000-odd weeks later, and I have enjoyed every one of them.
jokes.punchline: Mathematical specificity about enjoying every week of survival
jokes.explanation: Great character moment - dark backstory delivered with mathematical precision and relentless optimism.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: I'm not just positive. I have calculated the exact percentage of my life that I have left to live, and I am determined to enjoy every single week of it. By my calculations, I have approximately 2,847 weeks left. That's 40,276 days. So I'm going to enjoy every one of them.

jokes.text: Jurassic Park. Parks are so great. The Parks Department needs money. I just did it in three moves.
jokes.setup: Leslie claiming she can easily bring up Parks Department
jokes.punchline: Her terrible demonstration of conversation steering
jokes.explanation: Good Leslie moment - her complete lack of subtlety demonstrated with terrible conversation example.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: So anyway, like I was saying, the Parks Department is really important and—

dialogue.speaker: Other person
dialogue.line: Yeah, did you see the game last night?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Oh, sports! You know what's like sports? Parks. Parks are a level playing field where everyone can—

jokes.text: Well, then, why don't you just come here and do it yourself? / Okay. Holy crap!
jokes.setup: Ann suggesting Leslie come do it herself
jokes.punchline: Leslie immediately appearing at the restaurant
jokes.explanation: Good timing - Leslie's immediate appearance after suggestion. Physical comedy of her dedication.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: If you want it done right, why don't you just come do it yourself?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: You know what? I will.

jokes.text: They're both so beautiful. They probably just want to see each other naked.
jokes.punchline: His inappropriate and obvious observation
jokes.explanation: Tom's inappropriate but honest observation. Character-consistent crudeness but not particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Chris and Ann are totally going to get together.

jokes.text: Yes, I am. / No, you're not. / I'm great at being sneaky. / Clearly, you're not.
jokes.punchline: Immediate contradiction proving she's not sneaky
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Leslie's self-delusion about her subtlety immediately contradicted.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I'm actually really sneaky. I'm like a ninja.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Leslie, you're wearing a neon green jacket that says 'Parks Department' on the back.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: That's my disguise.

jokes.text: Yes, Ron Swanson is dating my ex-wife, Wendy. Big deal. My girlfriend, Lucy, is the sexiest woman in town. She's Cuban, she's got tattoos, and she's into me, which, as far as I'm concerned, is the sexiest quality a woman can have.
jokes.setup: Tom addressing Ron dating his ex-wife
jokes.punchline: His escalating descriptions ending with ultimate narcissism
jokes.explanation: Good Tom moment - his insecurity masked by bravado, ending with perfect narcissistic punchline.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Ron, you're dating my ex-wife? That's cool. I mean, it's a little weird, but it's cool.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Actually, it's pretty weird.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Okay, it's really weird.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Ron, you're dating my ex-wife. Do you understand what you're doing to me? I'm the one who has to see you two together.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I'm the victim here.

jokes.text: After dinner, we should take a walk by the pond in Ramsett Park. Walking in parks can be very romantic. / Yeah, too bad the park's always closed, though.
jokes.punchline: Chris pointing out the parks are closed due to budget cuts
jokes.explanation: Good irony - Leslie's park suggestion undermined by the budget cuts she's fighting against.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: We could take a romantic walk through the park this weekend.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: Actually, all the parks are closed due to budget cuts.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: What? No, that's... we have to do something about this.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: I know. It's devastating.

jokes.text: This place is outstanding! Great call, Leslie Knope.
jokes.punchline: His extreme enthusiasm for everything
jokes.explanation: Consistent Chris character moment but predictable. His enthusiasm for everything.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: This is literally the most incredible club I've ever been to in my entire life. The lighting design, the energy, the way the bass frequencies resonate through your thoracic cavity — it's a perfect ten out of ten. Actually, I'd say it's an eleven out of ten. No, a twelve. The DJ is phenomenal, the crowd is enthusiastic, and I'm having the time of my life right now. This might be the best night ever!

jokes.text: That's a foul! What? On whom? / Your team. Number 50. He was double dribbling. / He's on defense. / Exactly. / That's a technical difficulty.
jokes.punchline: Escalating nonsensical rules and terminology
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of Tom's basketball ignorance. 'Technical difficulty' is funny mispronunciation.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Okay, that's traveling. No wait, that's a foul. Actually, that's a technical foul. And now he's in violation of the Magna Carta.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Tom, what are you talking about?

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I don't know, I'm just making stuff up now. That guy just committed... high treason against the sport of basketball.

jokes.text: So, that means Andy's team throws the ball from the stripey thing.
jokes.punchline: Calling free-throw line 'the stripey thing'
jokes.explanation: Tom's sports ignorance perfectly captured in 'stripey thing.' Efficient and quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: That's a foul. No wait, the guy on the stripey thing is open.

jokes.text: I'm sort of a gay hero. Last year, I married two penguins at the zoo, and it turned out they were both gay.
jokes.setup: Chris claiming to be a gay hero
jokes.punchline: Accidentally marrying gay penguins
jokes.explanation: Great absurdist moment - Chris's earnest pride in accidentally performing gay penguin wedding.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: I'm basically a gay hero. I once accidentally married two gay penguins at the zoo.

jokes.text: Look. When I was 18 and I became mayor of my hometown, I used every last dollar we had to open a giant winter sports complex. Called it Ice Town. And it turned out great and everyone loved it. Yeah, kind of. It was never completed, and I got impeached.
jokes.setup: Ben telling success story about Ice Town
jokes.punchline: Gradual revelation it was a complete disaster
jokes.explanation: Good escalation and misdirection. Ben's attempt to sound competent completely backfires.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Ice Town was a great success. We had Ice Rinks, Ice Sculptures, the Ice Skating Lottery...

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Sure, we went bankrupt and had to lay off half the staff, but we really captured the magic of ice.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Okay, fine, it was a disaster. A complete disaster. I cost the town millions of dollars and everyone hates me.

jokes.text: The newspaper headline was, 'Ice Town Costs Ice Clown His Town Crown.' They were big into rhymes.
jokes.punchline: Ridiculous rhyming headline and Ben's deadpan observation about rhymes
jokes.explanation: Brilliant wordplay and perfect Ben deadpan delivery. Became iconic quotable moment. Excellent craft in rhyme construction.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: They're calling it 'Ben's Lyin' and Tryin'.'

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Which is ridiculous because those words don't even rhyme.

jokes.text: Foul on number three for taking a number two on number four.
jokes.punchline: Poop pun about player numbers
jokes.explanation: Juvenile poop pun but fits Tom's immaturity as referee. Cheap humor but character-appropriate.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Number 23, that's a solid player. Number 24? Also great. But Number 25? That guy's all clogged up.

jokes.text: What are you going to do about it? Nothing. You fouled. You can't do anything. / Okay. You're ejected. You're ejected. / What's the matter there, Ron? No players left? / Put my boys back in. / You made me the ref. Deal with it.
jokes.setup: Tom and Ron arguing about calls
jokes.punchline: Tom's power trip as referee escalating to ejecting everyone
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of Tom's power abuse and Ron's frustration. Character conflict drives humor.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I'm telling you, that was a foul!

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: That was a clean play, Haverford.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: You know what? I'm the ref now, and I say it's a foul. Technical foul. That's two shots.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: You can't just make up rules—

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Oh, I can't? Ejected. You're out of the game.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: And you—ejected. And you—ejected. Everyone's ejected. Game's over. I win.

jokes.text: Ron's ejected for molesting the ref!
jokes.punchline: Escalating to absurd 'molesting the ref' charge
jokes.explanation: Escalation of Tom's power trip to absurd accusation. Efficient but relies on inappropriate reference.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: You're out! You're out of here!

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: And you, sir, are accused of molesting the ref. Get out!

jokes.text: I dedicate this victory to April Ludgate. It feels good. And it feels sticky. From the Gatorade.
jokes.punchline: Romantic moment undercut by sticky Gatorade observation
jokes.explanation: Good Andy moment - romantic gesture immediately undercut by physical discomfort. Character-appropriate.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I want to dedicate this victory to April, the love of my life.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: That's sweet.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: You're so beautiful, and you smell like a sweet flower... mixed with something else. What is that?

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Gatorade. I've been drinking it all day and it's all over my hands.

jokes.text: Mission accomplished? / Yeah. There's a mission that Ann had thought of. It was both of our ideas, but it was mostly Ann's.
jokes.punchline: Her terrible attempt to throw Ann under the bus
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment - Leslie's horrible attempt to deflect blame while still taking credit.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ann, I want you to know that this was all Chris's idea. I was completely against it from the start.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: What? No, you came up with this!

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Chris is obviously confused. Ann, you know how he gets.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Leslie, I was literally in the room when you planned this.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ann's been under a lot of stress lately. I think she might be misremembering things.

jokes.text: I've just had my first bad date.
jokes.punchline: His devastation at having first bad date
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback to his earlier claim about never having bad dates. Chris's worldview shattered by deception.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: Wait, so you were just using me to get to Ben?

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: What? No, Chris, I—

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: I can't believe this. My first real date and it was all a setup. This is worse than when I tried to eat that entire rotisserie chicken at the Sweetums office party.

jokes.text: Really? Wow. Across the pond.
jokes.setup: April says she was in Venezuela
jokes.punchline: Andy thinking Venezuela is across the pond (like England)
jokes.explanation: Perfect Andy geography ignorance. Efficient character moment showing his obliviousness.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I was in Venezuela.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Venezuela? Isn't that across the pond?

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: No, Andy. It's in South America.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Oh. I thought it was near England or something.

jokes.text: This is my boyfriend, Eduardo. / No, it's not.
jokes.punchline: Eduardo's immediate contradiction
jokes.explanation: Brilliant economy - entire fake relationship destroyed in three words. Perfect deadpan timing.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: This is my boyfriend, Eduardo.

dialogue.speaker: Eduardo
dialogue.line: We're not dating.

jokes.text: How do you say, 'Have a great time, don't choke on anything'?
jokes.setup: Andy trying to be polite in Spanish
jokes.punchline: Bizarrely specific and inappropriate farewell
jokes.explanation: Perfect Andy - trying to be nice but ending up weirdly aggressive. Character-driven absurdity.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Adiós. I'm leaving now. Adiós, amigos.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: May your socks never get wet, and may you always find twenty dollars in your old jacket.

jokes.text: Budget solution number 28. Use grazing sheep to mow grass in parks. Note. Tired sheep could become food or sweaters.
jokes.punchline: Escalation to using tired sheep for food or sweaters
jokes.explanation: Great Leslie moment - practical solution that escalates to absurd sheep exploitation. Dark but character-appropriate.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: We could use tired sheep for food or sweaters.

jokes.text: From some city in Mexico.
jokes.punchline: Thinking Venezuela is a city in Mexico
jokes.explanation: Callback to earlier geography mistake. Consistent character ignorance but repetitive concept.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Thinking Venezuela is a city in Mexico

jokes.text: You swing the hardest, damn it. You go big or you go home. And you don't seem like the kind of guy who goes home. / I'm not. I don't even really have a home.
jokes.punchline: Andy's literal interpretation about not having a home
jokes.explanation: Good Andy moment - takes metaphor literally, revealing sad truth about his living situation.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: You need to believe in yourself. You need to have confidence that you can achieve anything you set your mind to.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: But I don't have a home.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: What? No, that's not what I meant—

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I've been living in a tent behind the community center for three weeks.

jokes.text: Check your testicles? / No. Not that. Although that is very good advice. Looking at you, Jerry.
jokes.punchline: Leslie's aside about Jerry needing to check his testicles
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection and typical Jerry targeting. Leslie's practical health advice mixed with comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Andy, the point is that you have to believe in yourself. You have to think positive thoughts and—

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Say no more. I'm going to go tell everyone that I'm the best dancer in the world.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: That's not... okay. Also, Jerry, you need to go check your testicles.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What? Why?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Just... go do it.

jokes.text: This town was historically known for two things. Widespread obesity, and the annual Pawnee Harvest Festival.
jokes.setup: Leslie listing the town's historical claims to fame
jokes.punchline: Pairing obesity with harvest festival as equal achievements
jokes.explanation: Good dark humor - Leslie's earnest pride in the town's obesity problem. Efficient world-building.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Pawnee was founded in 1823 by settlers who were looking for the flattest land possible. We succeeded. We also have the highest obesity rate in the state, and we're very proud of that. And, of course, the Harvest Festival.

jokes.text: People from all over Indiana would come and gaze in wonderment at how fat our citizens were.
jokes.punchline: People specifically came to marvel at obesity
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of obesity joke - Leslie's proud description of fat tourism. Dark but character-appropriate.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: People specifically came to marvel at obesity

jokes.text: That, literally, is the most moving thing I've ever heard.
jokes.punchline: His extreme emotional reaction to routine government presentation
jokes.explanation: Consistent Chris character moment - extreme emotional response to everything. Word 'literally' becoming his catchphrase.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: I'm not crying. You're crying.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: That was the most beautiful thing I've ever witnessed.

jokes.text: All due respect, Eduardo. You seem like a great guy. But I like April. And I'm coming after her with everything I've got.
jokes.punchline: His polite but aggressive declaration of romantic war
jokes.explanation: Good Andy moment - his earnest politeness combined with romantic determination. Character-appropriate directness.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Eduardo, I'm going to say this once, and I'm going to say it politely. April is mine. Back off.

dialogue.speaker: Eduardo
dialogue.line: Andy, she doesn't belong to anyone.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I know that. I'm not saying she's property. I'm saying that I love her, and I'm going to fight for her. Politely. But aggressively.

jokes.text: Not Friday. I have to go visit my cousin. But I will be back after that to ask you again.
jokes.setup: Andy promising to ask April out repeatedly
jokes.punchline: Specific exception for cousin visit in romantic persistence
jokes.explanation: Good Andy moment - his practical scheduling consideration in the middle of romantic declaration. Character-appropriate details.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I'm going to ask April out every single day until she says yes.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Every single day?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Every single day. Well, except when her cousin visits. I don't want to seem desperate.

jokes.text: You should do it. Follow your dream. / Oh, my God. Really? That's awesome.
jokes.punchline: Andy's positive interpretation of what was likely insult or dismissal
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection - Andy's optimistic interpretation of Spanish probably contradicts reality. Character-appropriate obliviousness.

dialogue.speaker: Eduardo
dialogue.line: Vete al diablo, idiota.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Wow! I have no idea what you just said, but it sounded beautiful. Was that Spanish? I love Spanish people.

tmdb_overview: Leslie and the Parks Department return to work after three months to discover that the budget crisis still looms. After unsuccessfully trying to have their budget restored by using Ann to charm Chris with a date, Leslie decides to make an all or nothing play by resurrecting the Pawnee Harvest Festival. Meanwhile, Ben restarts the youth basketball league and instates Ron and Andy to coach the only two teams. April returns from a vacation with a new boyfriend.
insight: Season 3 premiere resets with 73 jokes in 24 minutes—character-driven setup over laughs.
